author: Simon Rogers
themes: PDF extraction; Adobe Professional; Text Wrangler; HTML tables
extract: Tables on pages can sometimes be extracted using Adobe Professional (which has an option if you highlight the table and control-click it). Often it's simpler to use a basic programme like Text Wrangler to access the data. If a table's published on a web page, one thing worth trying is saving the web page as html -- and then opening it in Excel, which then opens it as a table.
relevance_rationale: Specific tool advice about Adobe Professional and Text Wrangler for PDF extraction is obsolete; LLMs handle this natively

author: Simon Rogers
themes: Google Charts; Google Fusion Tables; free tools; quick output
extract: At the end of that process is the output -- will it be a story or a graphic or a visualisation, and what tools will we use? Our top tools are the free ones that we can produce something quickly with. We commonly use Google charts for small line graphs and pies, or Google Fusion Tables to create maps quickly and easily.
relevance_rationale: Google Fusion Tables is discontinued; the specific free tool recommendations are entirely outdated

author: Paul Bradshaw
themes: self-directed learning; function discovery; search strategy
extract: You can start to search for useful functions whenever you come across a task or problem which you think others may have already solved, adding the word 'function'. For example, 'Excel function extract day from date' or 'Google spreadsheets function count blank cells'.
relevance_rationale: LLMs now serve as the primary way to discover spreadsheet functions rather than searching with keywords

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
themes: video verification; YouTube; reverse image search; Amnesty International
extract: Videos can be harder to find. Unlike photographs, there is currently no reverse search platform for videos, but Amnesty International's YouTube Dataviewer will extract the preview frames from a YouTube video, allowing them to be easily reverse image searched. However, it will not be accurate if the video is edited, shortened, or extended with additional footage.
relevance_rationale: AI video analysis and reverse video search tools now exist, making the no-reverse-search-for-video limitation obsolete

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
themes: text cleaning; URL removal; preprocessing
extract: What we want to do is to make the content column easier to analyze. ... We'll probably want to remove URLs from the text of the tweets since they aren't actual words.
relevance_rationale: Manual text preprocessing like URL removal and tokenization is handled automatically by LLMs
notes: Strip URLs before tokenising text; they add noise to word frequency counts

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
themes: tokenisation; case normalisation; punctuation removal; text preprocessing
extract: If you are trying to create a list of unique words, R will treat differences in capitalization as unique and also will include punctuation by default ... Fortunately, this is a solved problem with tidytext, which has a function called unnest_tokens that will convert the text to lowercase and remove all punctuation.
relevance_rationale: Case normalization and punctuation handling for text analysis is obsoleted by LLM text understanding
notes: unnest_tokens handles lowercase conversion and punctuation removal in one step

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
themes: stop words; text preprocessing; word frequency
extract: Well, that's a bit underwhelming - a lot of very common (and short) words. This also is a solved problem in working with text data, and words like 'a' and 'the' are known as 'stop words.' In most cases you'll want to remove them from your analysis since they are so common.
relevance_rationale: Stop-word removal as a manual step is obsoleted by LLMs that inherently understand word significance
notes: Remove stop words to surface meaningful content words in frequency analysis

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
themes: ngrams; bigrams; text analysis; multi-word phrases
extract: The next step in text analysis is using ngrams. An ngram is any combination of words that you specify. Two word ngrams are called bigrams (bi-grams). Three would be trigrams.
relevance_rationale: N-gram frequency analysis is largely superseded by LLM semantic understanding of text
notes: Move from single-word to multi-word analysis to capture phrases and richer patterns

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
themes: crowdsourcing; data-creation; PDF-extraction
extract: ProPublica created a software application to enable volunteers to translate unsearchable PDF files into structured data and sort the files by market, amount, candidate, and political group. Nearly 1,000 people pored over the files, logging detailed ad spending data to create a public database that otherwise wouldn't exist.
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing PDF-to-structured-data conversion has been largely replaced by LLM-powered document extraction and OCR
notes: Free the Files project

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
themes: tool-ecosystem; workflow-design
extract: The canonical suite of tools for computer-assisted reporting: spreadsheets, databases, text editors, and statistics software. Today, data journalists also leverage Web-based tools like Open Refine, Google Fusion Tables, and Tableau, and modern programming languages like Python, Ruby, and Javascript, as well as d3.
relevance_rationale: The canonical tool list (Excel, Access, Fusion Tables, etc.) is thoroughly outdated; LLMs plus modern tools have replaced most of this specific stack

author: Mike Reilley
themes: efficiency; transcription; workflow design
extract: An old reporter's trick: do not transcribe the entire interview. As you record, keep track of the time code on the recorder. In your notebook, list the times when an interesting quote starts or ends, and work forward or backward from there. This saves many hours of time with transcriptions.
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now transcribe full interviews cheaply and instantly, making selective time-code transcription unnecessary

author: Mike Reilley
themes: data scraping; spreadsheets; data collection
extract: You can scrape data from the FDIC Failed Banks List (or any other site with a table of data) with this basic scraping formula.
relevance_rationale: Basic table scraping formulas are now trivially handled by LLMs that can scrape, parse, and structure any web table

author: Mike Reilley
themes: AI tools; document analysis; reporting workflow
extract: The tool allows you to upload documents and analyze a document up to 75,000 words. You can upload multiple documents.
relevance_rationale: Specific tool for document upload and analysis is superseded by LLMs that natively analyze documents up to millions of tokens

author: Mike Reilley
themes: data scraping; financial data; monitoring; spreadsheets
extract: Business reporters use this to track companies and their stock prices across multiple markets.
relevance_rationale: Specific stock-tracking tool reference is superseded by LLM agents that can query financial data in real time

author: Sandra Crucianelli
themes: CSV; spreadsheets; data cleaning; file formats
extract: When a CSV data file is downloaded, the columns must be restructured using Text to Columns in the Data tab, selecting Delimited with Tab and Comma separators.
relevance_rationale: Manual CSV restructuring with Text to Columns is now handled automatically by LLMs and modern data tools that auto-detect delimiters

author: Sandra Crucianelli
themes: mashups; data integration; hybrid applications; web tools
extract: Mashups allow the combination of data that exists in different sources, such as YouTube, Flickr, Google Maps and Google Earth. The scenario in which mashups are developed is incredibly versatile and new resources appear every day.
relevance_rationale: Mashup tools like Flickr/Google Maps integration described here have been superseded by modern APIs and LLM-powered data integration

author: Sandra Crucianelli
themes: search skills; advanced search; data acquisition; monitoring
extract: Skills developed in web searching: using Google in the traditional way is not enough. One must learn to get more out of advanced searches, by format, by domain and by date; but also, in the absence of a national open data initiative, monitor the possible official sites where there is data.
relevance_rationale: Advanced Google search techniques by format, domain, and date have been largely supplanted by LLM-powered search and data discovery tools

source_title: Antecedentes y fundamentos del Periodismo de datos
author: various
themes: scraping; tools; data extraction
extract: This technique called scraping or in Spanish 'rascar' (to scrape) is carried out using applications such as: Kimono Labs, Scraper Wiki and Outwit Hub, which automatically extract information from an Internet portal in .xml format for subsequent analysis.
relevance_rationale: Specific scraping tools like Kimono Labs and ScraperWiki are defunct; LLM-assisted coding now handles web scraping tasks
notes: Scraping tools circa 2016. Kimono Labs now defunct; ScraperWiki renamed. Historically situated.

source_title: Antecedentes y fundamentos del Periodismo de datos
author: various
themes: toolstack; team structure; Excel; OpenRefine; Datawrapper; Tableau; D3
extract: the Data Unit is made up of a multidisciplinary team of three people: Daniele Grasso, Jesús Escudero and Adrián Blanco. They also have the support of developers, designers and layout artists from the media outlet. Among the computer applications they use for their daily work, the following stand out: Microsoft Excel, Open Refine, Datawrapper, Infogram and Tableau Public. For more complex visualizations and applications they use D3, a JavaScript library.
relevance_rationale: Specific toolstacks like Excel/OpenRefine/Datawrapper/D3 circa 2016 are dated; LLMs now handle many of these tasks directly
notes: El Confidencial data unit toolstack circa 2016. Core team of 3 plus support staff. Two-tier viz: simple (Datawrapper/Infogram/Tableau) vs complex (D3).

source_title: Antecedentes y fundamentos del Periodismo de datos
author: various
themes: toolstack; Tabula; CartoDB; OpenRefine; newsroom integration
extract: Among the tools they use to develop data journalism, the following stand out: Excel, OpenRefine, Tabula, Kimono Tableau, CartoDB and D3. As an independent section of the newspaper, they seek out their own topics, although they are permeable to current events and the topics being covered in the newsroom. Collaboration with the print newsroom is continuous.
relevance_rationale: Specific toolstacks citing Tabula/Kimono/CartoDB are historically situated and largely superseded by LLM-assisted workflows
notes: El Mundo data unit toolstack circa 2016. Adds Tabula and CartoDB. Notable: independent topic selection but continuous collaboration with print newsroom.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
themes: text cleaning; OCR; tokenization; NLP preprocessing; stopwords
extract: Cleaning: OCR. Filtering stuff out like documents in other languages. Lowercasing, punctuation, stripping HTML, bylines, etc. Tokenization (words -> columns). Stemming/lemmatization & part-of-speech tagging. Remove stopwords and meaningless words.
relevance_rationale: Manual text preprocessing (tokenization, stemming, stopword removal) is largely automated by LLMs that handle raw text directly

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
themes: TFIDF; clustering; sentiment analysis; word vectors; NLP techniques
extract: Counting words. TFIDF (Term Frequency Inverse Document Frequency). Keyword. Clustering. Sentiment. Vectorization. Vectorization = give it lots of documents, it'll figure out which words appear in similar contexts. Can ask analogies like what is the Republican version of what the Democrats call an estate tax.
relevance_rationale: TFIDF, manual clustering, and word vectors are largely superseded by LLM embeddings and zero-shot classification for journalistic text analysis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
themes: text preprocessing; stop words; NLP; data cleaning; tokenization
extract: We're going to remove 'stop' words, such as a and the. Then we'll remove all the punctuation and pronouns. Plus some garbage tokens that came along with the emails we have. Everything gets cleaned up with this reusable function.
relevance_rationale: Manual stop-word removal and text preprocessing is largely handled automatically by LLM embeddings that understand semantic meaning directly
notes: Standard NLP preprocessing pipeline: stop words, punctuation, pronouns, domain-specific garbage

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
themes: text chunking; sentence segmentation; semantic search; preprocessing
extract: We have to do this because pages and paragraphs often cover multiple topics, which might confuse the model. And, Universal Sentence Encoder is built to encode sentences... and so it ignores anything after the 128th word in its input. The code below cuts the text into sentences, but groups any two consecutive sentences under 15 words long together.
relevance_rationale: Sentence-level splitting for Universal Sentence Encoder is obsolete as LLMs handle full-document semantic search natively
notes: Chunking strategy must respect both model input limits and topic coherence

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
themes: whitespace cleaning; Python string methods; split-join pattern; data cleaning
extract: Remove internal whitespace: Python does not have a built-in string method to remove internal whitespace, but Googling around will yield a common strategy: Using the split() method to separate individual words in the string, then join()ing the resulting list on a single space. As a one-liner: my_text_clean = ' '.join(my_text.split()).
relevance_rationale: Manual internal whitespace removal via split/join is trivial for LLMs to handle in generated code without explicit instruction
notes: split-then-join idiom for normalizing internal whitespace

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
themes: character encoding; utf-8; latin-1; data cleaning; web scraping
extract: Fix encoding errors: You might have noticed a few encoding problems --- e.g., Nestle is being rendered as NestlA. This is due to an encoding problem --- the warn_page.text is not encoded as utf-8. Using decode() and encode(), see if you can fix this. Hint: It looks like the state of Maryland is a big fan of latin-1.
relevance_rationale: Manual encoding error diagnosis and fix via .encode/.decode is trivially handled by LLMs and modern libraries
notes: Encoding mismatches common in government web scraping; detect and convert

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: search operators; Google advanced search; background research
extract: The simple two-letter word 'is' reveals opinions and facts about your subject. To avoid clutter, include the company name of the person or any other detail you know, and tell Google that both words should not be that far from each other. The AROUND() operator MUST BE IN CAPITALS. It sets the maximum distance in words between the two terms.
relevance_rationale: Manual Google search operator syntax for finding people is largely displaced by LLM-powered search and research agents
notes: Henk van Ess technique for building background profiles using AROUND() proximity operator

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: government records; site operator; public records
extract: Search for worldwide official documents about this person. It searches for gov.uk (United Kingdom) but also .gov.au (Australia), .gov.cn (China), .gov (U.S.) and other governmental websites in the world. If you don't have a .gov website in your country, use the local word for it with the site: operator.
relevance_rationale: Manual site-specific government domain searching is displaced by LLM search tools that can query across jurisdictions conversationally
notes: Using site:gov.* variants to find official documents across jurisdictions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: name variations; iterative search; identity resolution
extract: With this formula you can find results that use different spellings of the name. You will receive documents with the word Shell, but not those that include 'Ben' as the first name. With this, you will find out that he is also referred to as Bernardus van Beurden. Now repeat steps a, b, c and d with this new name.
relevance_rationale: Boolean name-variant search formulas are displaced by LLMs that handle name variations, transliterations, and exclusions natively
notes: Iterative technique: discover name variants then re-run all previous search strategies with new spellings

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: search strategy; keyword selection; information retrieval
extract: Search engines like Google don't actually know what web pages are about. They do, however, know the words that are on the pages. So to get a search engine to behave itself, you need to work out which words are on your target pages. Choose your search terms wisely. Each word you add to the search focuses the results by eliminating results that don't include your chosen keywords. Some words are on every page you are after. Other words might or might not be on the target page. Avoid those subjective keywords, as they can eliminate useful pages from the results.
relevance_rationale: The epistemology of keyword-based search engines is being displaced by semantic search where LLMs understand page meaning, not just keywords
notes: Core principle: think about what words will appear on the target page, not what the page is about

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: search operators; OR operator; keyword strategy
extract: If you don't have definite keywords, you can still build in other possible keywords without damaging the results. Pages discussing heroin use in Texas might not include the word 'Texas'; they may just mention the names of different cities. You can build these into your search as optional keywords by separating them with the word OR (in capital letters).
relevance_rationale: Manual synonym/location keyword expansion is displaced by LLMs that natively understand geographic and linguistic variants
notes: Using OR for optional keywords when target pages may use synonyms or specifics instead of general terms

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: site operator; domain filtering; institutional sources
extract: You can focus your search on a particular site by using the search syntax 'site:' followed by the domain name. You can also use it to focus your search on municipal and academic sources, too. This is particularly effective when researching countries that use unique domain types for government and university sites.
relevance_rationale: Site-specific search operator syntax is displaced by LLM search agents that handle domain filtering conversationally

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: filetype operator; data formats; structured data
extract: Some information comes in certain types of file formats. For instance, statistics, figures and data often appear in Excel spreadsheets. Professionally produced reports can often be found in PDF documents. You can specify a format in your search by using 'filetype:' followed by the desired data file extension.
relevance_rationale: Filetype-based search operators are displaced by LLM tools that can locate and parse spreadsheets and PDFs directly
notes: Matching expected file format to data type: spreadsheets for stats, PDFs for reports

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: OpenRefine; data profiling; faceted browsing; histograms
extract: Open Refine is an excellent tool to quickly explore and evaluate the quality of databases. You can use Open Refine to run a numeric facet in the Amount field. A numeric facet groups numbers into numeric range bins. This enables you to select any range that spans a consecutive number of bins. You can generate a histogram with the values range included in the database. Records can then be filtered by values by moving the arrows inside the graph. The same can be done for dates and text values.
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine numeric faceting is now largely displaced by LLM-powered data profiling that can do richer quality assessment conversationally

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: username-search; cross-platform; namechk
extract: When I find a username or email I think might belong to my subject, I plug it into an online tool like namechk or namecheckr that looks for username availability across multiple platforms. These tools are designed to be an easy way for marketers to see if a given username they're planning to register is available across platforms. But they're also useful for checking whether a username you're investigating also exists elsewhere.
relevance_rationale: Specific tools like namechk/namecheckr are displaced by LLM agents that can check username availability across platforms programmatically
notes: Username match across platforms does not prove same person

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: Google-search; site-operator; primary-sources
extract: Go to a generic search engine. Start with the query and end with 'site:' followed directly with the URL (no spaces). This is the formula for finding out if an original source shows you everything.
relevance_rationale: Site-specific search operator syntax is displaced by LLM search tools that handle domain-scoped queries conversationally
notes: Primary source check formula: query + site:domain

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: Google-search; filetype-operator; document-trail
extract: Try to find the common denominator of any online invitation. It's often a PDF document. Search for just that with 'filetype:pdf' and you might find it.
relevance_rationale: Filetype-specific search operators are displaced by LLM agents that find and parse documents across formats conversationally
notes: Part of a 3-step document trail method: establish type, be neutral on wording, identify who is involved

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: Twitter; advanced-search; platform-specific-search
extract: Generic search engines like Google often struggle to deliver quality results from the trillions of posts on Twitter, or on big platforms such as Facebook and Instagram. The answer for Twitter is to use its Advanced Search functionality and add keywords, username and time period.
relevance_rationale: Platform-specific search tools like TweetDeck advanced search are displaced by LLM-powered social media analysis tools
notes: Google site: operator unreliable for social media platforms

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: Google-search; exclusion-operator; network-mapping
extract: Exclude the target website by adding -site:friendsofscience.org. This helps you find information from outside parties.
relevance_rationale: Site exclusion operators are basic search syntax now handled conversationally by LLM search agents
notes: Excluding target site reveals third-party references

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: Twitter-search; engagement-filter; viral-content
extract: You can filter by engagement simply by adding 'min_retweets:500' or 'min_faves:500' to your search query. This will get only tweets with at least 500 retweets or likes.
relevance_rationale: Platform-specific engagement filter syntax like min_retweets is displaced by LLM tools that query social data conversationally

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
themes: OpenRefine; data profiling; data quality
extract: Open Refine is an excellent tool to quickly explore and evaluate the quality of databases. Open Refine can be used to run a numeric 'facet' which groups numbers into numeric range bins. This enables you to select any range that spans a consecutive number of bins. The same can be done for dates and text values.
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine for data exploration is now largely superseded by LLMs that can profile and clean data conversationally

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
themes: Excel; VLOOKUP; data transformation; date parsing
extract: using advanced Excel formulas including VLOOKUP, RIGHT, LEFT, SEARCH to combine and format data; using Excel formulas to change contents of a cell into more useful formats (e.g. convert a date in the format of 2/13/2014 into 4 columns with a weekday, day, month, and year)
relevance_rationale: Specific Excel formulas like VLOOKUP, RIGHT, LEFT, SEARCH for data transformation are now handled more naturally by LLM-assisted coding
notes: Specific Excel formulas for restructuring data

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
themes: Google Refine; spelling correction; data standardization
extract: using Google Refine to correct spelling mistakes in hand-entered data
relevance_rationale: Google Refine for spelling corrections in hand-entered data is now largely handled by LLM-powered fuzzy matching and entity resolution

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
themes: pivot tables; Google Fusion Tables; TileMill; D3; interactive maps; visualization tools
extract: Microsoft Excel Pivot Tables, for large data set summaries; Google Fusion Tables to create basic charts and maps of data, providing HTML code that can be embedded into Web pages; TileMill, an online resource that allows users to design aesthetically pleasing interactive maps using a CSS-like class structure; Data-Driven Documents (D3), a JavaScript library for creating interactive (and often strikingly beautiful) visualizations.
relevance_rationale: Google Fusion Tables is defunct; TileMill is deprecated; the specific tool stack has been superseded by modern alternatives and AI-assisted viz
notes: Tool stack from simple (pivot tables) to complex (D3), scaffolded progression

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du
themes: environment setup; technical barriers; tooling
extract: Many programmers told me it's easy to master a code language, but you will give up at the first step — environment configuration. Without the configuration, you cannot install the software or the software cannot run well.
relevance_rationale: Environment configuration as a hidden barrier to entry is now largely eliminated by LLMs that can debug setup issues and cloud-based tools
notes: Alumnus A3 quote; environment configuration as a hidden barrier to entry for journalists learning technical tools

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
themes: data entry templates; constrained input; automation; accuracy
extract: By far, the most important strategy used to facilitate the coding process was electronic code response sheets. For each of the three coding parts, students received an Excel or Word document to record results in; these documents used functions like drop-down responses and automated data-checking processes to save time and improve accuracy.
relevance_rationale: Drop-down templates and automated data-checking in Excel are largely superseded by LLM-assisted data entry and validation
notes: Drop-downs and automated checks embedded in collection instruments
